techniques.evidence_quote: the hacking happened after someone gained access to their systems via a former employee’s account
the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account
the authentication code had been changed before executives attempted to log in
Hackers gained access to KiranaPro’s root accounts on AWS and GitHub
all their Electric Compute Cloud (EC2) services ... were deleted
we are not able to get any logs or anything because we don’t have the root account

attack_chain.description: A former (laid-off) employee used still-valid credentials tied to their account to access KiranaPro's systems, including AWS and GitHub.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the hacking happened after someone gained access to their systems via a former employee’s account

evidence_chunks.quote: the hacking happened after someone gained access to their systems via a former employee’s account
the hacking happened after someone gained access to their systems via a former employee’s account

attack_chain.description: The attacker changed/replaced the Google Authenticator MFA configuration on the AWS root account so legitimate users could no longer log in with their existing MFA code.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account last week

evidence_chunks.quote: the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account last week
the multi-factor code had changed when they tried to log into their AWS account last week

attack_chain.description: Attacker obtained root-level access to AWS and GitHub accounts belonging to KiranaPro, achieving full control over the cloud tenant and source code repositories.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Hackers gained access to KiranaPro’s root accounts on AWS and GitHub

evidence_chunks.quote: Hackers gained access to KiranaPro’s root accounts on AWS and GitHub
Hackers gained access to KiranaPro’s root accounts on AWS and GitHub

attack_chain.description: By taking over the root account and changing the MFA, the attacker locked legitimate KiranaPro staff out of root access, leaving them only able to use lower-privileged IAM accounts.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: We can only log in through the IAM [Identity and Access Management] account ... we are not able to get any logs or anything because we don’t have the root account

attack_chain.description: Attacker deleted all EC2 instances hosting KiranaPro's application servers, wiping app code and servers containing customer names, addresses, and payment details, causing service disruption.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: all their Electric Compute Cloud (EC2) services ... were deleted

attack_chain.description: Attacker also destroyed/wiped source code in the GitHub repositories, requiring restoration from an employee-held backup.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the GitHub data was restored using a backup provided by one of their employees

evidence_chunks.quote: the GitHub data was restored using a backup provided by one of their employees
the GitHub data was restored using a backup provided by one of their employees

initial_access_vector: A laid-off (former) employee used retained/valid account credentials to access KiranaPro's AWS and GitHub environments; the company had failed to revoke the ex-employee's access post-layoff.
persistence_mechanism: Attacker replaced the Google Authenticator MFA seed bound to the AWS root account, ensuring only they could reauthenticate as root while locking out legitimate staff.
privilege_escalation: Gained control of the AWS and GitHub root accounts (highest-privilege tier) after leveraging the former-employee account.
